title: DoorDash: great unit economics, but many unanswered questions
text: DoorDash spends approximately $6 to acquire each new customer. The company notes they spend upwards of 10% of GOV on sales and marketing in year one, with initial average order value around $30. DoorDash's gross margin in year one is approximately 2%, rising to about 12% by year three. Customer Lifetime Value (LTV) comes from delivery fees, commissions from restaurants, and subscription services like DashPass. Estimating a three-year post-acquisition value of about $21 yields a CLV of approximately $15, implying a marketing return on investment of 250% in just the first three years. Extending the horizon to five years increases the post-acquisition value to approximately $60, implying a CLV of $54, or a 900% marketing ROI. DashPass plays a critical role in improving LTV by dramatically increasing order frequency and customer retention. Optimization of unit economics involves negotiating better restaurant terms, optimizing delivery routes to lower costs, and increasing order volume through strategic partnerships.
